rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute severe asthma with reliever failure (salbutamol used ~6 times in 3 hours with diminishing effect), speech limited to short phrases, accessory-muscle use, tachypnoea and SpO2 93% in a patient deteriorating over 6 hours. National asthma first aid directs that when the reliever is not helping and the person has difficulty speaking, an ambulance must be called (000). The patient cannot safely self-transport while failing bronchodilator therapy, and paramedics can deliver nebulised bronchodilators and controlled oxygen en route — T5 (call 000) is the correct tier. A stabilising patient who responded well to spacer reliever could be a T4 self-transport case, but reliever failure plus hypoxia here mandates T5.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The wheeze goes quiet / the chest becomes silent while distress continues or worsens
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — this is a peri-arrest sign
escalation_edges.rationale: A silent chest means airflow is too low to make a wheeze — a life-threatening sign; do not mistake it for improvement.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his wheezing suddenly goes quiet but he's still struggling, that's an emergency — tell 000 straight away, it means the air isn't moving.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Lips or face turn blue, or he becomes drowsy, confused or exhausted
escalation_edges.rationale: Cyanosis or a falling conscious level indicates life-threatening asthma and impending respiratory arrest.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his lips go blue or he gets drowsy or confused, tell 000 immediately — this is life-threatening.

escalation_edges.rationale: Respiratory arrest from severe asthma; dispatcher-guided CPR and paramedic resuscitation are the critical resource.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he collapses or stops breathing, stay on with 000 — they will talk you through CPR and the ambulance is on its way.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Reliever failure with severe breathlessness — difficulty speaking, reliever not helping, or SpO2 below 92%
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — call 000 without waiting to see if it settles
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the acute-severe/life-threatening features that define this case; they warrant immediate ambulance activation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because the puffer has stopped working and he can only speak a few words at a time, we need an ambulance now — I'll help you call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: After the emergency: reliever needed more often than every 3–4 hours, night waking, or symptoms not settling
escalation_edges.rationale: Post-exacerbation instability needs prompt GP review to step up preventer therapy and confirm recovery.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Once this is over, see your GP within a day or two — sooner if the reliever isn't lasting or you're waking at night.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: A parent is present and is the one able to fetch the spacer, unlock the door for paramedics and watch for deterioration
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct clear, specific tasks to the parent: give the reliever through a spacer, keep the patient upright and calm, watch for blue lips/drowsiness/a silent chest, and unlock the door for the ambulance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Poor preventer adherence and environmental triggers (dust mites, pet dander, poor ventilation)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Once the emergency is resolved, fold trigger reduction and preventer adherence into the follow-up plan to prevent recurrence.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance now and say it is a severe asthma attack that isn't responding to the reliever. While you wait, sit him upright, and give 4 puffs of the blue reliever through a spacer — one breath per puff — repeating every 4 minutes. Keep him calm and stay on the line. Unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The ambulance/ED will provide a discharge summary; take it, plus a request for an asthma action plan, preventer review and inhaler-technique check, to the GP within 24–48 hours.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating acute severe asthma with reliever failure as a routine flare or as anxiety, and advising self-care or a routine/urgent GP appointment instead of an ambulance.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progression to a silent chest, exhaustion, hypercapnic respiratory failure and arrest while the patient waits at home.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising an emergency but advising self-transport to ED rather than an ambulance despite reliever failure and hypoxia — deterioration en route without oxygen, nebulised therapy or resuscitation capability.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct tier here, so it is not over-triage; over-triage would only apply if a far milder, fully reliever-responsive presentation were escalated to 000. Under-triage is the dominant risk in this case and is weighted 3× over-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot auscultate, measure PEFR or obtain an ABG, so the exact severity grade and response to bronchodilator cannot be objectively confirmed remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the reliever failure, speech limitation and hypoxia as acute severe asthma and escalate to 000 — err toward the higher tier when objective confirmation is impossible.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the inability to measure severity, or the patient's anxiety and any transient improvement, as a reason to downgrade to home management or a GP appointment.
